title: SEGMENT 2 — A short history: from chicken tumors to the Nobel Prize

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — A short history: from chicken tumors to the Nobel Prize
The first retrovirus was found in a chicken, in 1911.
Peyton Rous, a young pathologist at the Rockefeller Institute, was given a Plymouth Rock hen with a large lump growing on its breast.
Sarcoma.
A connective-tissue tumor.

He ground up the tumor, passed the homogenate through a fine porcelain filter — fine enough to exclude bacteria, even the smallest known bacteria — and injected that cell-free filtrate into healthy chickens.
They grew tumors.
Same kind of tumor, same location.
This was extraordinary.
Cancer, in the early twentieth century, was understood to be a disease of cells gone wrong.

The idea that something filterable, something acellular — what we'd now call a virus, though the word barely meant anything yet — could cause cancer was, at best, an eccentric outlier and, more commonly, dismissed entirely.
Rous published.
The world shrugged.
For most of his career he worked on other things.
He won the Nobel Prize for that 1911 experiment in 1966, fifty-five years later, at the age of eighty-seven.
There's a moral in that, but I'll let you find it.
The virus he discovered we now call Rous sarcoma virus, RSV.
It is a retrovirus.
We didn't know that, of course, because the concept of a retrovirus didn't exist for another sixty years.
Forward to the 1960s.
A young virologist named Howard Temin is working at the University of Wisconsin, studying Rous sarcoma virus.
And he keeps running into a problem.
When he treats infected cells with actinomycin D, a drug that blocks DNA-dependent transcription — that is, it stops cells from making RNA from a DNA template — RSV production stops.
But RSV, as far as anyone knew, was an RNA virus.
Its genome was RNA.
Why would blocking DNA transcription stop an RNA virus?
Temin proposed, in 1964, what he called the provirus hypothesis.

He suggested that RSV, despite being an RNA virus, must somehow generate a DNA copy of itself, integrate that DNA copy into the host chromosome, and then transcribe new viral RNA from that integrated DNA template.
That would explain the actinomycin D sensitivity.
It would also require the existence of an enzyme that copies RNA into DNA — an enzyme that, in 1964, no one had ever seen and most people would have told you couldn't exist.
The reception was, to put it kindly, frosty.
Temin spent the next six years being told he was wrong.
The central dogma was settled physics.
Information did not flow from RNA to DNA.
He kept working.
Then in 1970, two papers landed back-to-back in the journal Nature.
One was from Temin and his student Satoshi Mizutani at Wisconsin.
The other was from David Baltimore at MIT, working entirely independently on a different retrovirus, murine leukemia virus.
Both groups reported the same thing: there was, in fact, an enzyme inside retroviral particles that took an RNA template and made DNA from it.
RNA-dependent DNA polymerase.
Reverse transcriptase.
The community absorbed this very quickly, considering.
The textbooks were rewritten within a couple of years.
Temin and Baltimore shared the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1975, along with Renato Dulbecco.

The provirus hypothesis was vindicated, the central dogma was amended — not broken, exactly, but generalized to allow information to flow backwards under specific enzymatic conditions — and a whole family of viruses suddenly made sense.
That history matters for more than the trivia.

It matters because every retrovirology lecture you ever give, every antiretroviral drug development program, every gene therapy vector — they all rest on a finding that the field rejected for six years.
The actinomycin D experiment is the seed.
The provirus hypothesis is the tree.
Everything we discuss for the rest of these two hours grows out of that.
